ContentApiResponse.description:
Typed shape of ``format_content_for_api_response`` (``util.py``).

Serves the ``results`` payload of the GET and PATCH content operations. The
stable core fields below are always present; ``include_perms=True`` adds the
dynamic ``CONTENT_ENTITY_PERMISSIONS`` flags plus ``is_shared_with_project``,
and ``last_modified_by_*`` is present only when the content has a last
modifier. To document the stable core while tolerating those runtime-variable
keys (and to stay a named ``$ref`` rather than a bare ``dict``), the model is
``extra="allow"``.

This model is declared for docs only; the service dict flows through
``create_response`` unchanged (the tests compare against the raw
``format_content_for_api_response`` dict).
ContentApiResponse.title: ContentApiResponse

ContentPatchRequest.description:
Partial request body for ``PATCH /projects/{project_id}/contents/{content_id}``.

The legacy PATCH handler performed **no** body validation: it read the raw
``json.loads(request.body)`` and ``setattr``-ed every key onto the content
object (``tags`` handled separately). This model exists purely to give the
generated OpenAPI a named ``$ref`` documenting the fields a PATCH typically
carries, while preserving the legacy permissiveness:

  * every field is Optional (a PATCH may send any subset), and
  * ``extra="allow"`` so the arbitrary-field legacy contract is not tightened
    into a 422 (unknown keys still validate), and
  * ``description`` accepts an explicit ``None`` (the legacy path nulls the
    description when ``{"description": null}`` is sent).

IMPORTANT: this model is for docs + validation only. The Ninja adapter
forwards the **raw** ``json.loads(request.body)`` to the service (NOT
``model_dump()``), so the arbitrary-``setattr`` semantics and whitespace
stripping that live in the service layer are preserved byte-for-byte
(typed-for-docs / raw-for-behavior).
ContentPatchRequest.title: ContentPatchRequest

ContentSidenavItem.title: ContentSidenavItem

ContentTagMutationResponse.description:
Inner ``results`` payload for content-tag POST/PATCH mutations.

The legacy mutation results are heterogeneous:

  * create -> ``list[str]`` (all tag names after creation),
  * delete/edit -> ``{"success": bool, "message": str}``,
  * patch -> the single tag ``to_json()`` dict.

The list-vs-dict split cannot be a single object schema, so the operations
declare ``response=enveloped(...)`` with a loose named ref: this model
documents the ``{success, message}`` mutation-status shape (the most common
keyed result) and is ``extra="allow"`` so the spec stays an honest ``$ref``
rather than a bare inline ``dict``. The behavior path returns the raw inner
object unchanged, so the on-the-wire ``results`` value is byte-identical to
the legacy ``APIResponse(results=...)`` envelope.
ContentTagMutationResponse.title: ContentTagMutationResponse

ContentTagPatchRequest.description:
Partial request body for ``PATCH /projects/{project_id}/content-tags[/{tag_id}]``.

The legacy PATCH path forwarded ``json.loads(request.body)`` straight to
:meth:`ContentTagAPI.patch`, which pops ``name`` and re-validates it itself
(raising ``InvalidDefinitionError("Tag name must be defined")`` -> 400 when
``name`` is absent). To keep that contract:

  * ``name`` is Optional -- requiredness lives in the service, so a missing
    ``name`` yields the legacy 400, not a Ninja 422, and
  * ``extra="allow"`` so unknown keys are accepted (the legacy view ignored
    them), and
  * the router forwards the **raw** body, never ``model_dump()``.

Do NOT make ``name`` required or reuse a strict create model -- that would
convert the service-level 400 into a Ninja 422 and change behavior.
ContentTagPatchRequest.title: ContentTagPatchRequest

ContentTagPostRequest.description:
Polymorphic request body for ``POST /projects/{project_id}/content-tags``.

The legacy view performed **no** body validation: it forwarded
``json.loads(request.body)`` straight to :meth:`ContentTagAPI.post`, which
branches on the raw keys (``delete`` / ``edit``) and otherwise treats the
body as a create. The body is therefore a union-superset of three shapes:

  * create  -> ``{"name": ...}``
  * delete  -> ``{"delete": true, "name": ...}``
  * edit    -> ``{"edit": true, "tagName": ..., "newTagName": ...}``

This model exists purely to give the generated OpenAPI a named ``$ref``
documenting the polymorphic body (instead of "any JSON object"). To preserve
the legacy permissiveness it:

  * marks every field Optional (no single shape's fields are all present), and
  * uses ``extra="allow"`` so any currently-accepted body passes, and
  * is NEVER ``model_dump()``-ed back into the service -- the router forwards
    the **raw** ``json.loads(request.body)`` (the service pops/inspects keys
    like ``name``/``delete``/``edit``, so a round-trip is not identity).

Requiredness of any field lives in the service
(``InvalidDefinitionError`` -> 400), not in this schema, so a missing field
yields the legacy 400, not a Ninja 422.
ContentTagPostRequest.title: ContentTagPostRequest

ContentTagsGetResponse.description:
Inner ``results`` payload for ``GET`` content-tags endpoints.

The collection GET returns ``{"tags": [<name>, ...], "can_edit": bool}`` while
the item GET returns ``{"tags": <tag.to_json() dict>, "can_edit": bool}``, so
``tags`` is genuinely a union of a list-of-names and a single tag object.
``extra="allow"`` keeps the model an honest, open ``$ref`` should the tag
``to_json()`` shape grow without forcing a schema change here.
ContentTagsGetResponse.title: ContentTagsGetResponse

CreateBookmarkUrlRequest.description:
Request body for ``POST /api/app/projects/{project_id}/bookmark-urls``.

Mirrors the voluptuous ``BOOKMARK_URL_SCHEMA`` *as it applies at the API
boundary*: ``user_id`` / ``project_id`` are injected server-side (not request
fields), and ``workspace_id`` is accepted then discarded by the view.

Intentionally permissive (``extra="allow"``, no ``slug`` regex, ``params`` left
as ``dict``) so no input the legacy boundary accepted now 422s. The real
voluptuous validation runs later in ``utils.create_bookmark_url`` (mocked in the
HTTP tests), so the boundary itself did almost no request-shape validation. The
adapter forwards the raw ``json.loads(request.body)`` to the view; this model is

CreateConnectorRequestModel.description:
Request body for creating a connector.

Field shape mirrors the runtime swagger ``CreateConnectorRequest``
(``connector_type`` / ``label`` / ``connector_properties`` /
``category_properties``), purely for the OpenAPI ``$ref``.

ALL fields are Optional and ``extra="allow"`` even though ``connector_type``
is logically required: the legacy behavior is that a missing/invalid
``connector_type`` is rejected by the *swagger* class with its own
``ValueError`` message ("Invalid value for `connector_type`, must not be
`None`") via the in-view ``value_error`` handler -- a behavior the test suite
asserts byte-for-byte (``test_no_connector_type_returns_400``). If this model
marked ``connector_type`` required, Ninja would intercept the body with a
generic 400 ("Field required") *before* the view ran, changing that message
and the auth-vs-validation ordering. Keeping the model permissive lets every
request reach the view unchanged -- the view re-reads the raw body and runs
the swagger validation. Do NOT tighten.

FeatureFlagApiPatchPayload.description:
Partial request body for ``PATCH /feature-flags/{flag_id}``.

The PATCH handler historically performed **no** body validation and accepted
arbitrary partial updates, so this model exists purely to give the generated
OpenAPI a named ``$ref`` (instead of "any JSON object") documenting the fields
a PATCH may carry. To preserve the legacy permissiveness it:

  * marks every field Optional (a PATCH may send any subset), and
  * uses ``extra="ignore"`` so unknown keys are not rejected, and
  * keeps structural fields (``ruleset``, ``content_environments``) as loose
    ``dict`` rather than the strict create-time models, which would reject
    legitimate partial bodies.

Do NOT reuse the strict ``FeatureFlagApiPayload`` here -- its required fields
(``name``/``key``/``context``/``serving_method``/``ruleset``) would reject
partial PATCH bodies and change behavior.

asserts) and the per-item required ``replay_id``.

No ``max_length`` constraint is declared on ``replays``: this unit is
verbose-locked, so the body model must stay permissive. A Pydantic
``max_length`` here would make Ninja raise a 422 (remapped to a 400 with the

validator stays on the view and performs the real validation, emitting the
exact jsonschema-worded messages the tests assert. This model exists only so
the generated OpenAPI advertises a named ``$ref`` request body.

its own message ("Input should be a valid string"), pre-empting the legacy

PostPayRequest.description:
Request body for ``POST /api/app/payments/pay``.

This model exists purely to give the generated OpenAPI a named ``$ref``
(mirroring the ``PostPayRequest`` schema in ``api-spec.yml``) instead of an
inline ``type: object``. It is intentionally **permissive and docs-only**:

  * ``requestBody`` is optional (an empty ``{}`` body is a valid request --
    ``test_full_payment`` posts ``data={}``), so every field is Optional and
    the body itself is mounted with ``Body(default=None)``.
  * ``extra="allow"`` so unknown keys are never rejected.
  * the ``amount >= 50`` constraint from ``api-spec.yml`` is surfaced for docs
    only via ``json_schema_extra={"minimum": 50}`` -- it is **NOT** an
    enforced ``Field(ge=50)``. Declaring a typed ``Body`` param makes Ninja
    parse and validate the model; an enforced constraint would raise its own
    validation error (422 -> 400 envelope) *before* the legacy
    ``request_post_schema_to_req_data`` decorator runs, changing the bad-body
    wire shape (legacy returns a bare empty-body ``HttpResponseBadRequest``).
    Keeping the model permissive leaves the legacy JSON-schema validator as
    the sole enforcer; the router forwards the raw body untouched.

account_id.description: Optional account_id to charge, for staff requests. Default value is billing account from request

amount.description: Optional amount to charge, as an integer number of cents greater than or equal to 50 (minimum stripe charge). Defaults to the current account balance, although if a customer owes less than 50 cents, they will be charged 50 cents. Payment amount cannot exceed the current amount due on the account.

memo.description: Optional memo to store in the AdminAction model. Defaults to "User-Initiated Charge"

PostPayRequest.title: PostPayRequest

This Pydantic model exists only so the generated OpenAPI advertises a named

+ all-Optional fields) so Ninja never rejects a body itself (which would
raise a 422 and shadow the legacy verbose 400). The adapter forwards the raw
``json.loads(request.body)`` to the view; nothing here coerces service input.

Fields mirror ``api-spec.yml`` field-for-field. ``time_comparison`` was an
inline ``type: object`` with no inner shape in the original JSON-schema; it is
read-only opaque (used only as a cache-key component via
``DashboardCardCacheParam.TIME_COMPARISON``), so it stays
``Optional[Dict[str, Any]]`` on this one nested field (pre-existing; flagged
for review). The either/or constraint (exactly one of ``card_id`` /
``bookmark_id`` / ``benchmark_card_id``) is enforced in the view body, not
here -- OpenAPI cannot express it and adding a validator would change the 400
path.

PublicDashboardMetadataPatchRequest.description:
Partial request body for ``PATCH .../public-dashboards/metadata``.

The metadata PATCH handler historically performed **no** body validation: it
reads ``body.get("is_enabled")`` and only mutates when the value is not
``None``, so a PATCH with an empty/partial body is a valid no-op. This model
exists purely to give the generated OpenAPI a named ``$ref`` documenting the
one field a PATCH may carry. To preserve the legacy permissiveness it:

  * marks ``is_enabled`` Optional (a PATCH may omit it), and
  * uses ``extra="ignore"`` so unknown keys are not rejected.

The Ninja adapter forwards the raw ``json.loads(request.body)`` to the impl,
which already treats both an absent key and an explicit ``None`` as a no-op,
so behavior is byte-identical to the legacy view.
PublicDashboardMetadataPatchRequest.title: PublicDashboardMetadataPatchRequest

PublicDashboardPasswordRequest.description:
Request body for ``POST .../public-dashboards/{b58_id}/password``.

Sets (or replaces) the password protecting a public dashboard link. The
service reads the raw value and runs ``validate_password`` on it, returning a
400 list of error strings when the password is too weak -- that is NOT a
framework validation error, so the Ninja adapter forwards the raw
``json.loads(request.body)`` to the impl and this model exists purely to give
the generated OpenAPI a named ``$ref`` instead of an inline object.

The DELETE method on the same path carries no body, so this model is attached
only to the POST operation.
PublicDashboardPasswordRequest.title: PublicDashboardPasswordRequest

PublicDashboardVerifyPasswordRequest.description:
``POST /verify/<uuid>/`` request body.

There is no legacy schema for this endpoint; the view reads
``json.loads(request.body).get("password", None)`` directly. A missing or
empty password is a legitimate "wrong password" -> 401 path (not a 422/400),
so ``password`` is **Optional** -- making it required would convert the
"no password supplied" case from a 401 into a 422/400, a behavior change the
cookie tests assert against.

Docs-only and permissive (``extra="allow"``): this model exists only so the
generated OpenAPI advertises a named ``$ref`` request body. The adapter
forwards the raw ``json.loads(request.body)`` to the view (in fact it just
re-dispatches to the unchanged view, which reads ``request.body`` itself), so
Ninja never validates or coerces the body.
PublicDashboardVerifyPasswordRequest.title: PublicDashboardVerifyPasswordRequest

Success ``results`` for ``GET /api/app/projects/{project_id}/rca/status``.

The view returns ``APIResponse({"status": ..., "messages": [...], "error":
...})``, wrapped by the success envelope as
``{"status": "ok", "results": {"status": ..., "messages": [...], "error":
...}}``.

``status`` is one of ``running`` / ``completed`` / ``failed`` (kept as a
free ``str`` to avoid tightening the contract). ``messages`` items are
opaque, agent-defined message objects pulled verbatim from Redis, so the
element type is left as ``Any``. ``error`` is ``None`` while running.

Request body for ``POST /api/app/projects/{project_id}/rca/trigger``.

Documents the body as a named ``$ref`` so the generated OpenAPI advertises a
real schema instead of an inline ``type: object``.

Every field is intentionally ``Optional`` (and no cross-field rules are
expressed here): the legacy view performs its own validation and returns a
*tracked* ``400`` (firing ``track_rca_trigger_failed(stage=VALIDATION)``)
for the "exactly one of ``bookmark_id``/``bookmark_url_slug``" and
"``rca_date`` is required" cases. Tightening any of these in Pydantic would
short-circuit Ninja to its own validation path and skip that instrumentation.

``metric_index`` is likewise typed loosely on purpose: the view rejects a
bool with a tracked 400 (``isinstance(metric_index, int) and not
isinstance(metric_index, bool)``). The adapter forwards the **raw**
``json.loads(request.body)`` rather than ``model_dump()`` so that bool/int
distinction reaches the view unmodified -- this model is for docs only.

RcaTriggerResponse.description:
Success ``results`` for ``POST /api/app/projects/{project_id}/rca/trigger``.

The view returns ``APIResponse({"dashboard_id": int, "task_id": str})``,
which the success envelope wraps as
``{"status": "ok", "results": {"dashboard_id": ..., "task_id": ...}}``. The
dedup-cache-hit path returns the identically-shaped cached payload at 200.
RcaTriggerResponse.title: RcaTriggerResponse

ValidateProjectCredentialsRequest.description:

forbids an inline ``dict`` / ``type: object`` body). Both fields are typed as

inline ``try/except`` (``KeyError`` -> "Missing param: project_token",
``API.DoesNotExist`` -> "Invalid API secret", ``Info.DoesNotExist`` ->
"Invalid project token"). If ``project_token`` were declared required here,
Ninja would raise its own validation error (remapped to a generic 400) before
the impl runs, changing the "Missing param: project_token" / "Empty body"
messages. Keeping the fields optional lets the impl stay the single source of
truth for error semantics while still publishing a named request schema.

The router forwards the **raw** ``json.loads(request.body)`` (not
``model_dump()``) so the impl's ``body["project_token"]`` ``KeyError`` path is
preserved byte-identically.

Wrapped by ``enveloped(ValidateProjectCredentialsResult)`` so the OpenAPI
documents the success envelope as
``{"status": "ok", "results": {"status": "success"}}`` -- byte-identical to
the legacy ``APIResponse(results={"status": "success"})``.

were required here, Ninja would reject the body first (422->400) and change
the error message/shape. Keeping them Optional preserves the in-view 400
branch verbatim. The router forwards the raw body to the impl, so this model
is docs/validation only.

WorkspaceCreateRequest.description:
Request body for ``POST /projects/{project_id}/workspaces``.

Typed-for-docs mirror of the voluptuous ``CREATE_WORKSPACE_SCHEMA`` in
``util.py``. The impl receives the **raw** ``json.loads(request.body)`` (the
legacy ``workspaces_api`` decorator validates it via voluptuous and injects

change behavior.
WorkspaceCreateRequest.title: WorkspaceCreateRequest

WorkspaceMembersRequest.description:
Request body for the workspace member mutation endpoints.

Serves both ``POST .../{workspace_id}/upsert-members`` and
``POST .../{workspace_id}/delete-members`` -- the two share one voluptuous
schema (``UPSERT_WORKSPACE_MEMBERS_SCHEMA == DELETE_WORKSPACE_MEMBERS_SCHEMA``
in ``util.py``). Typed-for-docs only: the impl's ``USERS_KEY in body`` /
``TEAMS_KEY in body`` membership checks are the behavioral contract, so the
impl receives the **raw** ``json.loads(request.body)`` injected by the legacy
``workspaces_api`` decorator.
WorkspaceMembersRequest.title: WorkspaceMembersRequest

WorkspacePatchRequest.description:
Request body for ``PATCH /projects/{project_id}/workspaces/{workspace_id}``.

Typed-for-docs mirror of the voluptuous ``PATCH_WORKSPACE_SCHEMA`` in
``util.py``: an all-Optional partial body except the required ``id``. The
impl's ``KEY in body`` membership checks are the behavioral contract, so the
impl receives the **raw** ``json.loads(request.body)`` (injected by the
legacy ``workspaces_api`` decorator via voluptuous) -- this model only shapes
the OpenAPI ``$ref``.

``data_filters`` is validated server-side by the backend ``DATA_FILTER_SCHEMA``
(and an external validation call); it is typed loosely here so the docs do not
constrain the wire shape while voluptuous still does the real validation.
WorkspacePatchRequest.title: WorkspacePatchRequest
